title: Capillary electrochromatography using a fluoropolymer as the chromatographic support material
abstract:
Capillary electrochromatography using a
fluoropolymer as the chromatographic support
material
Fused-silica capillary columns were packed with ethylene chlorotrifluoroethylene
(ECTFE) particles for use in capillary electrochromatography (CEC). Electroosmotic
flow (EOF) was generated in these columns using acetonitrile-water mixtures as the
mobile phase. Electroosmotic mobilities of 1.6  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1 (linear velocities of
1 mm s±1) were observed using a mobile phase without an electrolyte present. The
EOF in the ECTFE-packed columns is enhanced when using trifluoroacetic acid (TFA)
as a mobile phase additive; electroosmotic mobilities of 3.65  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1 (linear
velocity of 2.5 mm s±1) were observed. This enhancement of EOF is attributed to
dynamic coating of the ECTFE particles by TFA. Other electrolytes (i.e., Tris/Tris-HCl
buffer and H3PO4) in the mobile phase did not have such an enhancement of EOF.
However, a slight enhancement of EOF is observed, for example, if small quantities of
TFA are added to the mobile phase containing Tris buffer. The potential of ECTFE for
CEC is demonstrated by separating a mixture of amino acids.
Keywords: Capillary electrochromatography / Fluoropolymer / Electroosmotic flow / Ethylene
chlorotrifluoroethylene
EL 3251
Rafael Alicea-Maldonado
Luis A. Colón
Department of Chemistry,
State University of New York
at Buffalo, Buffalo, NY, USA
1 Introduction
There are few reports in the literature using fluoropoly-
mers as packing materials for liquid chromatography. The
existing reports have shown separation of pharmaceutical
compounds, polyaromatic hydrocarbons, substituted aro-
matic hydrocarbons, DNA fragments and proteins [1±8]. A
review of the subject was published by Danielson et al.
[9]. The low surface energy of fluoropolymers can reduce
the nonspecific adsorption typically seen in silica-based
materials. This can be favorable in the separation of
biomolecules (e.g., amino acids, peptides, and proteins).
In addition, fluoropolymers will not dissolve in high pH
mobile phases; this is not the case for most silica-based
materials. Fluoropolymers are a class of polymers in
which some or all of the hydrogen atoms of the C±H
bonds have been replaced by fluorine; these include
polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE), polyvinylidene fluoride
(PVDF), polyvinylfluoride (PVF), perfluoroalkoxy (PFA),
and the copolymers fluorinated ethylene propylene (FEP),
polychlorotrifluoroethylene (PCTFE), ethylene tetrafluoro-
ethylene (ETFE), and ethylene chlorotrifluoroethylene
(ECTFE). Fluoropolymers are insoluble in most common
solvents and resistant at any pH, providing excellent
hydrolytic stability. Most fluoropolymers are hydrophobic
and can potentially be used as the stationary phase for
separations. It is also possible to chemically modify
fluoropolymers to impart a desired surface characteristic
[10, 11]; this translates into different selectivity for
chromatographic use. These fluoropolymers can poten-
tially be used in capillary electrochromatography.
Capillary electrochromatography (CEC) is a microsepara-
tion technique that combines features of high perform-
ance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and capillary
electrophoresis (CE) [12, 13]. The separation of com-
pounds can be achieved by differential partition between
two phases, usually a stationary and a mobile phase,
differential electromigration, or a combination of both. As
a miniaturized separation technique, low solvent con-
sumption, low sample-volume requirements, increased
mass sensitivity, high separation efficiencies, and low
operational costs characterize CEC. CEC can be per-
formed in either open tubes or packed columns. Electro-
osmosis, rather than pressure, is used to transport the
mobile phase and the solutes through the capillary
column. Silica-based reverse phase materials are most
commonly used in CEC; however, the use of other
materials has been reported [14±22]. Because solvent
movement is achieved by electroosmosis, CEC allows the
study of materials that are not traditionally used in HPLC.
We can now explore materials with a variety of
selectivities. To this end, we are investigating the use
of fluoropolymers for CEC. The results of initial inves-
tigations on the use of a fluoropolymer, ECTFE,
[(CH2CH2CF2CFCl)x] as a packing material for CEC are
reported here.
Correspondence: Dr. Luis A. Colón, Department of Chemistry,
State University of New York at Buffalo, 578 Natural Sciences
Complex, Buffalo, NY 14260-3000, USA
E-mail: lacolon@buffalo.edu
Fax: +716-645-6963
Abbreviations: CEC, capillary electrochromatography; ECTFE,
ethylene chlorotrifluoroethylene
 WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, 1999
0173-0835/99/0101-0037 $17.50+.50/0
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
37
CE and CEC
 2 Materials and methods
2.1 Equipment
The CEC runs were performed in a system constructed in
our laboratory consisting of two cylindrical capsules with
an internal carousel that can hold 9 vials (Fig. 1). Each
end of the capillary is situated in a vial in the capsule. Both
capsules were pressurized with nitrogen at 80 psi. The
electric field was applied using a 0±30 kV high voltage
power supply (Company Whitehouse Station, NJ). Sam-
ple injection was performed at the anodic end of the
column by electromigration. On-column UV detection at
220 nm was performed using a Spectra 100 UV-Vis
detector from Thermo Separation Products (San Jose,
CA).
2.2 Reagents
The acetonitrile used to prepare the mobile phase was
HPLC grade and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St.
Louis, MO; Milwaukee, WI). The mobile phase additive,
trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) was obtained from Aldrich
(Milwaukee, WI). The dansylated amino acids used as
probe compounds and the sodium salt of TFA were also
purchased from Sigma. Methyl alcohol (HPLC grade) was
obtained from Spectrum (New Brunswick, NJ). Phos-
phoric acid (85%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific
(Pittsburgh, PA). Tris buffer (pH 7.8) was prepared using
tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and the Tris-HCl salt,
both obtained from Aldrich. Acetone used as an unre-
tained compound and EOF marker was purchased from
Mallinckrodt, Baker (Paris, KY). The water used in the
experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q UV Plus water
purification system, fed from a Milli-RO 10 Plus reverse
osmosis unit from Millipore (Bedford, MA). The mobile
phase was filtered through a 0.45 mm Nylon membrane
(Scientific Resources, Eatontown, NJ) and degassed prior
to use. ECTFE particles, 10 mm ID (Ausimont, Thorofare,
NJ) were generously donated by Integument Technolo-
gies (Rochester, NY).
2.3 Column packing
Fused-silica capillaries (Polymicro Technologies, Phoe-
nix, AZ) of 100 mm ID were used for the study. The total
length of the columns used in this study was 42 cm, of
which 27 cm were packed with the fluoropolymer.
Detection was performed immediately after the retaining
frit. Three steps were followed to pack a capillary: (i)
formation of outlet frit, (ii) packing with ECTFE particles,
and (iii) formation of the inlet frit. The outlet retaining frit
was fabricated by packing a portion of the column with
silica particles that were fused in place with a heating
38
R. Alicea-Maldonado and L. A. Colón
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
Figure 1. Scheme of the CEC system
used.
Figure 2. Apparatus used to pack the capillary columns
with ECTFE.

HPLC grade and purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St.
Louis, MO; Milwaukee, WI). The mobile phase additive,
trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) was obtained from Aldrich
(Milwaukee, WI). The dansylated amino acids used as
probe compounds and the sodium salt of TFA were also
purchased from Sigma. Methyl alcohol (HPLC grade) was
obtained from Spectrum (New Brunswick, NJ). Phos-
phoric acid (85%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific
(Pittsburgh, PA). Tris buffer (pH 7.8) was prepared using
tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane and the Tris-HCl salt,
both obtained from Aldrich. Acetone used as an unre-
tained compound and EOF marker was purchased from
Mallinckrodt, Baker (Paris, KY). The water used in the
experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q UV Plus water
purification system, fed from a Milli-RO 10 Plus reverse
osmosis unit from Millipore (Bedford, MA). The mobile
phase was filtered through a 0.45 mm Nylon membrane
(Scientific Resources, Eatontown, NJ) and degassed prior
to use. ECTFE particles, 10 mm ID (Ausimont, Thorofare,
NJ) were generously donated by Integument Technolo-
Fused-silica capillaries (Polymicro Technologies, Phoe-
nix, AZ) of 100 mm ID were used for the study. The total
length of the columns used in this study was 42 cm, of
which 27 cm were packed with the fluoropolymer.
Detection was performed immediately after the retaining
frit. Three steps were followed to pack a capillary: (i)
formation of outlet frit, (ii) packing with ECTFE particles,
and (iii) formation of the inlet frit. The outlet retaining frit
was fabricated by packing a portion of the column with
silica particles that were fused in place with a heating
R. Alicea-Maldonado and L. A. Colón
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
Figure 1. Scheme of the CEC system
Figure 2. Apparatus used to pack the capillary columns
element. After flushing out the excess of particles at both
sides of the outlet frit, the column was packed with
ECTFE. The particles were dispersed in methanol and
placed in the packing assembly shown in Fig. 2. The
assembly was sonicated while applying pressure with
nitrogen gas (~ 200 psi). Once the column was packed,
the inlet frit was fabricated by dipping the inlet of the
packed column in a solution of hydrolyzed tetraethox-
ysilane (TEOS) (i.e., sol-gel solution) purchased from
United Chemicals Technologies (Horsham, PA). Then the
column was heated to 60oC and the polymerized TEOS
fixed the fluoropolymer in place.
ECTFE is a fluoropolymer with high hydrophobicity. It is
important that the material is properly wetted before use.
To ensure wetability of the ECTFE material, we rinsed the
packed column with a mixture of acetonitrile/propanol (50/
50 v/v) and then with the mobile phase before installation
into the CEC apparatus. Once the column was installed,
the system was allowed to run under CEC conditions for
ECTFE does not possess ionizable moieties. However,
the use of capillary columns made of fluoropolymers in CE
has been documented [23±25]. The generation of EOF in
these columns was attributed to the adsorption of ions to
the surface of the plastic material. We investigated the
behavior of the ECTFE fluoropolymer in acetonitrile-
containing mobile phases. Since it has been shown that
EOF can be supported in silica-based materials without
adding electrolytes to the mobile phase [26], we inves-
tigated the generation of EOF in an ECTFE-packed
column without adding an electrolyte to the mobile phase.
Solvent flow is indeed possible in the ECTFE-packed
column under such conditions. As shown in Fig. 3, the
electroosmotic mobility is dependent on the acetonitrile
concentration in the mobile phase. It is clearly noticeable
that the highest linear velocities were obtained with
mobile phases containing 80% and 90% acetonitrile.
Although EOF was present, its magnitude was reduced by
about half with acetonitrile concentrations higher than
95%. Using 90% acetonitrile, the electroosmotic mobility
between columns averaged 1.5 ( 0.2)  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1
for the columns used in this study. The flow generated in
these columns can be attributed to several factors, all of
which act in synergism to contribute to the observed flow.
The walls of the capillary columns are fused-silica, where
EOF can be generated. The retaining frits in the column
are also silica-based. In addition, there is a portion of the
capillary tube that is not packed (i.e., 15 cm); the EOF
generated in the unpacked section (at the end of the
column) can act as a pump, pulling mobile phase through.
The difference in flows between the packed and
unpacked section of the column in CEC has been
addressed recently by Rathore and Horvµth [27]. They
showed that in a packed column, the electric field strength
and the flow velocity are not the same along the column;
there is a contribution to flow due to the open segment.
Another source of electroosmosis in our columns can be
attributed to impurities present on the fluoropolymer
material and/or adsorption of small quantities of ions that
can give rise to negatively charged sites.
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
CEC in a fluoropolymer-packed column
Figure 3. Electroosmotic mobility as a func-
tion of acetonitrile concentration in the mobile
phase. Running conditions: packed fused-
silica capillary, 100 mm ID  42 cm total length
(27 cm packed bed); applied voltage, 25 kV.
Error bars indicate  standard deviation.
TFA has been used as a mobile phase additive in HPLC
separations of polypeptides, including fluorocarbon-type
of packing [8]. Using a mobile phase of acetonitrile/water
(90/10 v/v), we studied the effect of TFA on the EOF in the
ECTFE-packed column. As depicted in Fig. 4, the
electroosmotic mobility is enhanced with the addition of
TFA to the mobile phase. The mobility observed
compares to that obtained with reversed phase silica-
packing materials used in CEC [12]. Using the mobile
phase with the highest concentration of TFA (0.15%), the
linear velocity showed a linear increase as a function of
the applied voltage. The electrical current also scaled
linearly with the applied voltage. The behavior shown in
Fig. 4 is intriguing since a decrease in velocity would be
expected as the concentration of electrolyte is increased,
due to a decrease in the double layer thickness. We
attribute the EOF enhancement to a dynamic coating of
the particles by TFA. This is a result of the fluorinated
nature of TFA, which can interact with the ECTFE
particles through fluorine-fluorine interactions. Therefore,
the anion of TFA adsorbed around the particles can
impart a negative charge on the material's surface and
hence the increase in surface charge will lead to an
increase in EOF. At higher concentration of electrolytes
and with the surface saturated with TFA, however, we will
expect to see a decrease in the EOF since the ionic
strength would be high enough to decrease the thickness
of the double layer, hence the electroosmotic mobility.
Such an effect can be seen in Table 1.
Table 1 shows the electroosmotic mobility obtained with a
mobile phase containing 90% acetonitrile and 10% water
for several electrolytes in the mobile phase, including TFA
and Tris. The mobilities observed in an open tube and in
an ECTFE-packed column are also shown. Note that the
mobility in the open tube is slower (2.21  10±4 cm2 V±1
s±1) than that in the ECTFE-packed column (3.65 
10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1). Typically, the flow in a packed bed is
R. Alicea-Maldonado and L. A. Colón
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
Figure 4. Influence of TFA on the electro-
osmotic mobility with a 90/10 v/v acetonitrile/
water mobile phase. Running conditions as in
Table 1. Electroosmotic mobilities in an acetonitrile/water (90/10 v/v) mobile phase using
0.15% (20 mM) TFA (in an open tube)
slower than in an open tube [12, 13]. This also supports
the idea that TFA adsorbs at the surface to increase the
EOF. In addition, Table 1 indicates that there is a
difference in electroosmotic mobility between TFA alone
and the other electrolytes. When the Tris buffer with
0.05% TFA is added to the mobile phase, the electro-
osmotic mobility showed a slight increase (0.27 to 0.37 
10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1). Although significant, the increase is
small due to the presence of Tris, which increases the
ionic strength, hence decreasing the thickness of the
double layer. When the concentration of Tris is reduced to
10 mM, keeping the concentration of TFA constant, the
mobility increased as expected (due to a decrease in ionic
strength). H3PO4 (100 mM) rendered a pH similar to that
of 0.15% TFA (~ 1.6) and was used for comparison
purposes. As can be seen the electroosmotic mobility
observed was small compared to that obtained with TFA.
The ionic strength, however, plays a role here.
We investigated the effect of pH on electroosmotic
mobility with a mobile phase containing 10 mM Tris and
a fixed concentration of the sodium salt of TFA (0.05%
trifluoroacetate). The pH was lowered by adding 0.05%
TFA. The electroosmotic mobility at three different pHs
was as follows: pH 8.3, 0.55 ( 0.01)  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1;
pH 6.3, 0.49 ( 0.01)  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1; pH 2.2, 0.96 (
0.02)  10±4 cm2 V±1 s±1. The electroosmotic mobility
increased as the pH was lowered. The increase can be
attributed to the higher hydronium ion mobility compared
3.4 Separation of dansylated amino acids
In addition to an increase in the EOF, TFA can also play
an active role in the separation process. Figure 5 shows
electrochromatograms of three dansylated amino acids
separated in the ECTFE column at different TFA
concentrations. Separation in an open tubular column
under similar conditions was also attempted for compar-
ison purposes (Fig. 5A). The highest resolution was
achieved in the ECTFE-packed column when using
0.15% TFA (corresponding to 20 mM). The amino acids
test compounds should be positively charged in the TFA
solutions and we can expect electrophoresis to be
involved in the separation process. As seen in Fig. 5,
however, the components are not completely resolved
when the sample was injected into the open tube capillary
column under identical separation conditions. This is an
indication that the separation in the packed columns is
possible because of the presence of the fluoropolymer.
The fact that the positively charged amino acids elute
after the EOF marker is also indicative of chromato-
graphic interaction with the ECTFE material. Note that the
electroosmotic mobility in the open tube was lower than
that in the ECTFE-packed column (see Table 1).
Separation of the amino acids was also attempted using
the other above-mentioned electrolytes in the mobile
phase. However, the peaks were not completely resolved.
This is the first attempt to use fluoropolymers as support
materials for CEC. We demonstrated that EOF could be
generated in capillary columns packed with ECTFE, using
acetonitrile-water mobile phases. The addition of TFA
enhances the EOF by dynamically coating the surface of
the fluoropolymer, imparting a negative charge. The
separations presented here were performed on columns
packed with unmodified ECTFE particles, using amino
acids as model compounds to investigate the potential of
this material for CEC. Modification of ECTFE with a
desired moiety should impart the selectivity required for a
Electrophoresis 1999, 20, 37±42
CEC in a fluoropolymer-packed column
Figure 5. Electropherograms of a mixture of three amino
acids at different concentration of electrolyte (TFA) in the
90/10 v/v acetonitrile/water mobile phase. (A) Open tube,
(B) 0.05 % TFA, (C) 0.10 % TFA, (D) 0.15 % TFA. Peak
identification: 1, glycine; 2, glutamic acid; 3, leucine.
Separation conditions: packed fused-silica capillary,
100 mm ID  42 cm total length (27 cm packed bed);
applied voltage, 25 kV; UV detection at 220 nm.
We thank Tim Koloski from Integument Technologies for
providing the ECTFE particles and helpful discussions.
